summary:
The Islamic Conquest of Persia (633–654 AD) was the destruction of the [[event:2059|Sassanid Empire]] by the armies of the [[event:2126|Rashidun Caliphate]] — one of the most consequential conquests in history, eliminating a state that had existed in various forms for over 400 years and transforming the heart of the ancient world. The decisive engagement was the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah (636 AD), where Arab Muslim forces under Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas defeated the main Sassanid army; the subsequent fall of the Sassanid capital Ctesiphon and the death of the last Sassanid emperor Yazdegerd III (652 AD) completed the conquest.

The conquest of Persia was transformative for both civilizations. Persian culture, rather than being extinguished, profoundly shaped the emerging Islamic civilization: Persian administrative practices, court culture, literary traditions, and intellectual achievements were absorbed and became central to Abbasid Islamic civilization. The Persian language survived (unlike other conquered languages that were replaced by Arabic) and eventually became one of the major literary languages of the Islamic world, giving rise to a rich tradition of Persian Islamic poetry (Rumi, Hafez, Omar Khayyam). The Persian conversion to Islam was eventually nearly complete, but Persians maintained a distinct cultural identity within the Islamic world — one that would express itself through the Shia branch of Islam (which has been Iran's state religion since 1501) and through the continuous cultural tradition now called Iranian civilization.
